learning_objectives: Understand the role of analytics in modern game development and live operations
Identify key performance indicators (KPIs) for different game business models
Set up an analytics pipeline from event collection to dashboard visualization
Distinguish between descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics
readings: Game Analytics: Maximizing the Value of Player Data Ch. 1-3
Unity Analytics Documentation and Best Practices

lecture_notes.introduction: Data-driven design does not mean letting numbers dictate every decision. It means making informed decisions with evidence rather than assumptions. The best game designers use analytics as a lens that sharpens their creative vision, not as a replacement for it. This course teaches you to collect, analyze, and act on player behavior data responsibly and effectively. We begin with the fundamentals: what data to collect, how to structure your collection pipeline, and what metrics actually matter for your specific business model. A premium console game and a free-to-play mobile game share almost no important KPIs in common. A premium game cares about completion rate, metacritic score, and sequel purchase intent. A F2P game cares about retention curves, conversion rates, and lifetime value per user. Understanding which metrics apply to your game and why is the first step toward data literacy. Throughout this course, we will also grapple with the ethical dimension: data collection requires informed consent, analysis should serve both business goals and player welfare, and the power to influence player behavior through design creates responsibility.
key_concepts: The analytics pipeline: instrumentation -> collection -> storage -> analysis -> action feedback loop
Key metrics by business model: premium (units sold, refund rate, completion rate, playtime), F2P (DAU, MAU, ARPDAU, LTV)
Event taxonomy: design events (gameplay actions), progression events, business events, error events, custom events
Analytics platforms: Unity Analytics, GameAnalytics, Amplitude, Mixpanel, custom data warehouse solutions
Data privacy fundamentals: GDPR (EU), COPPA (US children), CCPA (California), and global compliance requirements
Descriptive vs diagnostic vs predictive analytics: knowing what happened, why, what will happen, and what to do
Data quality: the garbage-in-garbage-out principle and why event validation matters more than event volume
Analytics team roles: data analyst, data engineer, data scientist, and how they collaborate with game designers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up Unity Analytics in a sample project, defining custom events for first-time user experience tracking, and viewing real-time data in the dashboard
setup_requirements: Unity 2023.2+ with Analytics package installed
GameAnalytics free account for platform comparison
Python 3.8+ with pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and scipy installed
Spreadsheet software for data analysis exercises

review.value: Instrument events in game code -> Collect via SDK -> Store in data warehouse -> Analyze with queries and dashboards -> Act on insights to improve the game. Each stage can introduce data quality issues.
DAU (daily active users), D1/D7/D30 retention, session length, sessions per day, conversion rate, ARPDAU (average revenue per daily active user), LTV (lifetime value per user).
A structured hierarchy for categorizing tracked player actions. Well-designed taxonomy makes analysis possible; poorly designed taxonomy creates an unusable data swamp.
European regulation requiring informed consent for data collection, right to access and delete personal data, and clear privacy policies. Violations carry fines up to 4% of global revenue.

learning_objectives: Build and interpret player retention curves using cohort analysis
Identify drop-off points using funnel analysis techniques
Design targeted interventions to improve retention at critical stages
Calculate lifetime value from retention and monetization data
readings: Deconstructor of Fun: Mobile Game Retention Benchmarks
Nir Eyal: Hooked - How to Build Habit-Forming Products Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Retention is the single most important metric in games. A game that retains players can grow through word-of-mouth and eventually monetize sustainably. A game that fails to retain players will fail regardless of acquisition spend or monetization design. The retention curve tells you the story of your game's relationship with its players: how many fell in love on day one, how many stayed through the first week, and how many became long-term residents of your game world. Today we learn to read these curves like a doctor reads vital signs -- each shape tells a diagnostic story. A curve that drops steeply on D1 but flattens by D7 suggests a rough onboarding experience but a strong core loop. A curve that drops steadily without flattening suggests the core loop itself lacks depth. We will build cohort retention curves from real data, identify the primary drop-off points, and design specific interventions targeting each stage. We will also learn the critical relationship between retention and lifetime value: improving D1 retention by even a few percentage points has a multiplicative effect on every subsequent metric because it increases the population of players who reach D7, D30, and beyond.
key_concepts: Retention curves: D1, D7, D30 retention as percentage of installing users who return on that specific day
Industry benchmarks: D1 > 40% good for mobile, D7 > 25%, D30 > 10%; PC/console benchmarks differ significantly
Funnel analysis: mapping the player journey from install to first session to first purchase to long-term engagement
FTUE (First Time User Experience) optimization: the first 5 minutes determine D1 retention more than any other factor
Re-engagement mechanics: push notifications, daily rewards, social obligations, content cadence, lapsed player campaigns
Cohort analysis: grouping players by install date to compare retention across different game versions or acquisition sources
Rolling retention vs classic retention: classic = returned on exactly day N, rolling = returned on day N or later
Churn prediction: using early behavior signals (session 1 actions, tutorial completion, day 2 return) to identify at-risk players
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a real retention dataset from a published mobile game: building cohort retention curves in Python, identifying the primary drop-off point, and calculating LTV from retention and ARPDAU data
setup_requirements: Sample retention dataset (provided on course portal)
Python with pandas and matplotlib for cohort analysis
Analytics dashboard with funnel visualization capability
LTV calculation spreadsheet template

review.value: Healthy games show steep early drop (D1), flattening curve (D7-D14), and stable long-term tail. If the curve never flattens, the core loop has a fundamental engagement problem.
Improving D1 retention from 30% to 40% is a 33% increase in your entire addressable audience for every subsequent metric. FTUE optimization has the highest ROI of any retention work.
Comparing retention curves across cohorts grouped by install date, acquisition source, or game version to identify what changes improved or harmed player engagement.
Using machine learning on early behavior signals to identify players likely to churn, enabling targeted re-engagement before they leave.

learning_objectives: Design statistically valid A/B tests for game features and monetization changes
Calculate required sample sizes and interpret results with confidence intervals
Avoid common pitfalls in game experimentation that lead to false conclusions
Implement a test framework that supports sequential experiments
readings: Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments Ch. 1-4 by Ron Kohavi
King.com Engineering Blog: A/B Testing at Scale in Mobile Games

lecture_notes.introduction: Intuition is unreliable. A/B testing replaces guessing with measurement. But a badly designed test is worse than no test at all, because it gives you false confidence in a wrong conclusion. The game industry is full of decisions that feel right but measure wrong: a flashier tutorial that improves D1 retention but tanks D7 because it set unrealistic expectations, a price drop that increases conversion rate but decreases total revenue because the same spenders just pay less. A/B testing, done correctly, protects you from these traps. Done incorrectly, it leads you into worse ones. Today we learn the statistical foundations that separate valid experiments from cargo-cult data science. We will design an A/B test from hypothesis through sample size calculation, run it on simulated data, and interpret the results with proper confidence intervals. We will also cover the most dangerous pitfalls: peeking at results before the test reaches statistical power, running too many variants and finding false positives through multiple comparison, and ignoring novelty effects that inflate short-term metrics for any change.
key_concepts: A/B test design: hypothesis statement, control group, treatment group, primary metric, guardrail metrics, success criteria
Statistical significance: p-values, confidence intervals, and understanding that p < 0.05 means 1-in-20 chance of false positive
Sample size calculation: effect size, statistical power (typically 80%), and how these determine required test duration
Common pitfalls: peeking at results early, testing too many variants, ignoring novelty effects, survivorship bias
Practical considerations: segment interactions, network effects in multiplayer games, long-term vs short-term metric divergence
Sequential testing: methods that allow early stopping while controlling false positive rate (always valid p-values)
Metric selection: choosing primary metrics that directly measure the hypothesis vs proxy metrics that correlate imperfectly
Test velocity: building systems that support rapid experimentation while maintaining rigor
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing an A/B test for a tutorial change: stating the hypothesis, calculating required sample size with the power formula, running the test on simulated player data, and interpreting results with confidence intervals
setup_requirements: Python with scipy.stats for statistical calculations
A/B test sample size calculator (Evan Miller online tool)
Simulated player behavior dataset for hands-on exercises
Jupyter notebook for interactive analysis

review.value: n = (Z_alpha/2 + Z_beta)^2 * (p1(1-p1) + p2(1-p2)) / (p1 - p2)^2. For a 5% baseline conversion rate and 1pp MDE at 80% power, you need approximately 7,500 users per group.
Metrics you monitor to ensure you are not causing harm while optimizing the primary metric. A tutorial change that improves D1 retention but tanks D7 retention is a net negative.
Testing 20 metrics simultaneously means one will appear significant at p<0.05 by chance alone. Apply Bonferroni correction or designate a single primary metric pre-registration.
Any change temporarily boosts engagement because it is new. Measure the steady-state effect by running tests long enough for novelty to wear off (typically 2+ weeks).

learning_objectives: Apply clustering algorithms to segment players by behavioral patterns
Build predictive models for player churn and conversion probability
Design personalized game experiences based on player segment identification
Evaluate the ethical implications of behavioral prediction and personalization
readings: Game Analytics: Maximizing the Value of Player Data Ch. 8-10
Machine Learning for Game Developers (selected chapters, provided)

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all players are the same, and treating them as a homogeneous mass leads to design decisions that serve no one well. Behavioral analytics reveals the distinct player archetypes within your game through their actions rather than their demographics. A whale and a minnow may share the same age and location, but their play patterns -- session frequency, progression speed, social engagement, spending triggers -- diverge dramatically. Understanding these behavioral segments allows you to design experiences that serve each group's needs and motivations. Today we apply clustering algorithms to real player behavior data to discover natural segments. We then build simple predictive models that estimate conversion probability and churn risk based on early-session behavior. These models power the personalization systems that modern live-service games use to tailor difficulty, offers, and content recommendations. We also confront the ethical line between personalization that improves player experience and manipulation that exploits psychological vulnerabilities. This distinction is not academic -- it is the difference between a sustainable business and a regulatory target.
key_concepts: Player segmentation: grouping players by behavior patterns (session frequency, progression speed, social activity, spending) rather than demographics
Clustering algorithms: k-means, hierarchical clustering, and DBSCAN applied to player feature vectors
Feature engineering for player behavior: deriving meaningful features from raw event data (session count, actions per session, time between sessions)
Bartle's player types (Achiever, Explorer, Socializer, Killer) as a framework, and its limitations for modern analytics
Churn prediction models: logistic regression and random forests using early behavioral signals to flag at-risk players
Conversion prediction: estimating the probability that a free player will make their first purchase based on engagement patterns
Personalization systems: dynamic difficulty, targeted offers, content recommendations based on player segment
Ethics of personalization: the line between improving experience (recommending content they will enjoy) and exploitation (targeting vulnerable spenders)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running k-means clustering on a player behavior dataset in Python to discover natural segments, then building a logistic regression churn prediction model and evaluating its accuracy with ROC curves
setup_requirements: Python with scikit-learn, pandas, and matplotlib
Player behavior dataset with 30-day activity history (provided)
Jupyter notebook for interactive analysis
Clustering visualization tools

review.value: An unsupervised algorithm that groups data points into k clusters by minimizing within-cluster variance. Applied to player data, it reveals natural behavioral archetypes without predefined categories.
Transforming raw event logs into meaningful numerical features (average session length, days since last play, purchase frequency) that algorithms can use for segmentation and prediction.
Receiver Operating Characteristic curve plotting true positive rate against false positive rate. AUC (area under curve) of 0.5 = random guessing, 0.8+ = good model, 1.0 = perfect prediction.

The test: would a fully informed player consent to this personalization? Recommending content they will enjoy passes. Showing larger store discounts to players predicted to quit (to prevent churn) passes. Targeting high spenders with exclusive limited-time offers designed to trigger FOMO fails.

learning_objectives: Design monetization systems for free-to-play games that generate sustainable revenue
Analyze the economics of whale-dependent vs broad monetization models
Evaluate the monetization mechanics of commercially successful F2P games
Calculate key monetization metrics and build revenue projection models
readings: Free-to-Play: Making Money From Games You Give Away by Will Luton Ch. 5-8
Deconstructor of Fun: Monetization Deep Dives (selected articles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Free-to-play is a business model, not a game design philosophy. The best F2P games create genuine value for players at every spending tier, including zero. The worst exploit psychological vulnerabilities for short-term revenue at the cost of player trust and long-term viability. The economic reality of F2P is stark: typically only 2-5% of players ever spend money, and a small fraction of those spenders generate the majority of revenue. This concentration creates a dangerous dependency on a tiny population of high-spending players. The industry is gradually shifting toward broader monetization models -- battle passes, cosmetic shops, season subscriptions -- that aim to monetize 10-20% of players at lower price points, creating more stable and sustainable revenue. Today we deconstruct the monetization systems of published F2P games, analyze their economic models, and design our own systems that balance revenue generation with player respect. We will build revenue projection models that help you evaluate whether a monetization design is financially viable before investing development resources.
key_concepts: Monetization models: cosmetic-only (Fortnite), convenience/time-saving (Clash Royale), content expansion (Genshin Impact), battle pass hybrid
The whale problem: when 0.15% of players generate 50% of revenue, your survival depends on a tiny, potentially vulnerable population
Broad monetization: battle passes, cosmetics, and low-price-point offers that monetize 5-15% of players instead of 1-2%
Value perception: players must feel they received fair value for every purchase, or they churn with negative word-of-mouth
Monetization pacing: when to surface first purchase opportunity, optimal price ladders from $0.99 to $99.99
Currency systems: hard currency (purchased), soft currency (earned), premium currency conversion rates, and inflation management
Subscription models: Netflix-style all-access passes, VIP tiers, and content drip strategies
Revenue metrics: ARPDAU, ARPPU, conversion rate, average transaction value, and how they combine to determine total revenue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deconstructing the monetization systems of three F2P games: one ethical broad-monetization model, one whale-dependent model, and one battle pass hybrid, with revenue modeling for each
setup_requirements: Three F2P games installed for live analysis (provided list)
Revenue modeling spreadsheet template with formulas
Monetization deconstruction framework worksheet
Calculator for ARPDAU and LTV computations

review.value: Broad: 10% of players pay, average $10/month, stable revenue. Whale-dependent: 1% of players pay, average $100/month, volatile revenue. Industry trend is toward broad monetization for sustainability.

The first purchase is the hardest. Offer a starter pack at 80% perceived discount for $0.99-$4.99. Once a player has made one purchase, the psychological barrier to future purchases drops dramatically.
Average Revenue Per Daily Active User. Total daily revenue divided by DAU. A healthy mobile F2P game targets $0.05-$0.15 ARPDAU depending on genre.
A game system that removes currency from the economy to prevent inflation. Without sinks, accumulated currency makes future purchases feel less valuable and reduces spending motivation.

learning_objectives: Design live operations cadences that sustain long-term player engagement
Balance in-game economies using sink-source analysis and simulation
Plan content seasons and events that drive both retention and revenue
Monitor live game health using real-time dashboards and alert systems
readings: Live Operations Handbook (provided course reading)
GDC Talk: 'Running a Live Game for 10 Years' (selected case studies)

lecture_notes.introduction: Launching a game is no longer the finish line -- it is the starting line. Modern live-service games generate the majority of their lifetime revenue after launch through ongoing content updates, seasonal events, and live operations. Running a live game requires a different skillset than building one: you must simultaneously maintain the existing experience, deliver new content on a predictable cadence, respond to player feedback and emerging issues, and optimize the game's economy and monetization without destabilizing what already works. The in-game economy is particularly critical and fragile. Every currency earned, every item purchased, every reward granted is part of an interconnected system where changing one value can cascade unpredictably through the entire economy. Inflation devalues existing player investments. Deflation makes progression feel impossibly slow. Balance errors can crater revenue or alienate the player base overnight. Today we learn the frameworks for managing live economies: source-sink analysis, simulation modeling, and the monitoring dashboards that let you detect problems before they become crises.
key_concepts: Live operations calendar: daily login rewards, weekly challenges, monthly events, quarterly seasons, annual anniversaries
Content cadence: the schedule and cost of producing new content to maintain engagement between major updates
Economy source-sink analysis: mapping all currency inflows (sources) and outflows (sinks) to identify imbalance risks
Economy simulation: spreadsheet or code models that project currency accumulation over time for different player archetypes
Event design: limited-time content that creates urgency, rewards engagement, and drives monetization without exploitation
Real-time monitoring: dashboard KPIs to watch daily (DAU, revenue, crash rate), weekly (retention cohorts, economy health), monthly (LTV trends, churn segments)
Incident response: what to do when an economy exploit is discovered, a pricing error goes live, or a content update tanks retention
Season pass design: structuring seasonal content to reward both time and spending without making either feel mandatory
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an economy simulation spreadsheet that models currency accumulation for casual, core, and whale player archetypes over a 90-day period, identifying when each archetype runs out of progression motivation
setup_requirements: Economy simulation spreadsheet template
Live game dashboard examples (provided screenshots and sample data)
Python for Monte Carlo simulation of economy scenarios
Case study materials from published live-service games

review.value: Sources add currency to the economy (daily rewards, quest completion, purchases). Sinks remove it (upgrades, cosmetics, repair costs). If sources exceed sinks, inflation occurs. If sinks exceed sources, progression stalls.
The regular schedule of new content delivery. Players expect predictable updates (weekly challenges, monthly events, quarterly expansions). Missed cadence erodes trust and increases churn.
When players accumulate currency faster than meaningful sinks consume it, prices must increase or new sinks must be introduced, devaluing earlier player investment.
Limited-time events create excitement and urgency. But if the only way to obtain a desirable item requires logging in every single day for 30 days, the urgency becomes obligation and resentment.

learning_objectives: Evaluate monetization mechanics against ethical frameworks and player welfare standards
Understand current and emerging regulations on loot boxes, gacha, and real-money trading
Design monetization systems that comply with regulations across major global jurisdictions
Identify and avoid dark patterns in monetization design
readings: UK Gambling Commission Report on Loot Boxes and Gambling-Like Mechanics
FTC Workshop Report: Inside the Game - Unlocking Consumer Issues Surrounding Loot Boxes

lecture_notes.introduction: The game industry's monetization practices are under increasing regulatory scrutiny worldwide. Multiple countries have enacted or proposed legislation targeting loot boxes, gacha mechanics, and in-game spending by minors. Designers who understand the ethical and legal landscape will build sustainable businesses that survive regulatory changes. Those who ignore it face fines, forced redesigns, and reputational damage that can destroy a studio. The core tension is real: randomized rewards create excitement and drive revenue, but they also share structural similarities with gambling. A loot box that costs real money and delivers random rewards of varying rarity triggers the same variable-ratio reinforcement schedule that makes slot machines addictive. Whether this constitutes gambling legally depends on jurisdiction, but whether it causes harm to vulnerable players is an empirical question that the research increasingly answers affirmatively. Today we audit monetization systems against both regulatory requirements and ethical principles. We identify dark patterns -- design choices that manipulate players into spending against their own interests -- and propose alternatives that maintain revenue while respecting player autonomy.
key_concepts: Loot box mechanics: random rewards, variable ratio reinforcement schedule, and the structural comparison to gambling
Regulatory landscape: Belgium (banned paid loot boxes), Netherlands (case-by-case), China (probability disclosure required), Japan (kompu gacha banned), US/UK (under review)
Age rating implications: PEGI and ESRB flags for in-game purchases and random paid items affecting store visibility and audience
Dark patterns in monetization: artificial scarcity, manufactured urgency, social pressure mechanics, sunk cost exploitation, confusionary currency
Ethical framework: would a reasonable person, fully informed about the odds and mechanics, still freely choose to make this purchase?
Probability disclosure: legal requirements to display drop rates and the practical implementation in storefronts
Spending limits and parental controls: platform-level and game-level tools for managing spending behavior
Industry self-regulation: ESRB commitment to loot box labeling, Apple/Google store policies, and their effectiveness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Regulatory compliance audit of a hypothetical game's monetization system: identifying violations across five jurisdictions and proposing compliant redesigns that preserve revenue intent
setup_requirements: Regulatory reference sheet covering major jurisdictions (provided)
Sample monetization design document for audit exercise
Dark pattern identification checklist
Revenue impact calculator for measuring cost of compliance changes

review.value: Cosmetic-only loot boxes with disclosed odds and no trading (lowest risk) to power-granting loot boxes with undisclosed odds and real-money trading (highest risk, likely regulated as gambling in multiple jurisdictions).

A design choice that manipulates user behavior against their interests. In games: confusionary currency (making real cost hard to calculate), artificial scarcity timers, social pressure spending, and sunk cost escalation.
A guaranteed reward after N unsuccessful attempts, capping the maximum cost to obtain a desired item. Required in China, increasingly expected by players globally. Reduces worst-case spending.
Disclose all probabilities, include pity/mercy systems, no paid advantages in competitive modes, parental controls and spending limits, age-appropriate design, comply with platform store policies.

learning_objectives: Calculate and optimize cost per install (CPI) across marketing channels
Build attribution models that connect marketing spend to player quality metrics
Design and evaluate creative assets using performance marketing data
Balance user acquisition spending against lifetime value for profitable growth
readings: Eric Seufert: Freemium Economics Ch. 3-6
AppsFlyer Mobile Attribution Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: User acquisition (UA) is where analytics meets marketing. Every dollar spent acquiring a new player must ultimately return more than a dollar in lifetime revenue for the game to be sustainable. This sounds simple but the reality is extraordinarily complex: marketing channels have different costs, different player quality, and different attribution windows. A player acquired through an Instagram ad for $2.50 might have a LTV of $1.80, while a player acquired organically through word-of-mouth costs nothing and has a LTV of $4.20. The UA analyst's job is to measure these differences, allocate budget toward the highest-quality channels, and continuously optimize creative assets to lower costs and improve player quality. Today we build the analytical framework for performance marketing: calculating true CPI across channels, building attribution models that connect ad exposure to game installs, measuring return on ad spend (ROAS) at different time horizons, and understanding the feedback loop between acquisition quality and game health. We also discuss the privacy changes (Apple ATT, Google Privacy Sandbox) that are fundamentally reshaping mobile game marketing by limiting tracking capabilities.
key_concepts: Cost per install (CPI) calculation: total channel spend divided by attributed installs, benchmarked by platform and genre
LTV to CPI ratio: the fundamental profitability equation. LTV/CPI > 1 means profitable growth; typical target is LTV/CPI > 1.5
Attribution models: last-touch, multi-touch, probabilistic matching, and how privacy changes affect each approach
Return on ad spend (ROAS): measuring revenue generated per dollar of marketing spend at D7, D30, D90, and D365
Creative optimization: A/B testing ad creatives, measuring click-through rate (CTR), install rate, and post-install quality
Channel mix: balancing paid acquisition (Facebook, Google, Unity Ads, TikTok) with organic growth (ASO, content marketing, community)
Apple ATT and SKAdNetwork: how iOS privacy changes limit tracking and require new measurement approaches
Organic multiplier: the ratio of organic installs generated per paid install, measuring word-of-mouth amplification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a UA performance dashboard that tracks CPI, ROAS, and LTV/CPI ratio across three marketing channels, identifying the optimal budget allocation
setup_requirements: Marketing performance dataset with channel-level spend and install data (provided)
Python or Excel for ROAS calculation and budget optimization
Attribution model comparison worksheet
Creative performance tracker template

review.value: LTV / CPI = return on each acquired user. Below 1.0 = losing money on every install. 1.0-1.5 = marginal. Above 1.5 = healthy growth margin that accounts for operational costs.
Return On Ad Spend. D7 ROAS of 15% means that by day 7, you have recouped 15% of the marketing spend for that cohort. D30 ROAS targets vary by genre but 30-50% is common.

iOS 14.5+ requires user consent for cross-app tracking. Opt-in rates average 25-35%, dramatically reducing the data available for attribution and targeting, forcing reliance on aggregated and modeled data.
For every paid install, how many organic installs does the game receive? A multiplier of 1.5 means 50% more organic installs than paid, effectively reducing true CPI by 40%.

class_schedule.topic: Data-Driven Design Decisions and Revenue Modeling
learning_objectives: Build comprehensive revenue projection models for different monetization strategies
Use analytics data to inform game design decisions without over-indexing on metrics
Balance player experience quality with business sustainability requirements
Apply sensitivity analysis to identify which assumptions most affect financial outcomes
readings: Eric Seufert: Freemium Economics Ch. 7-10
GDC Talk: Data-Driven Design Is Not Soulless Design

lecture_notes.introduction: Data tells you what players are doing. Design intuition tells you why. The best game designers use both, treating analytics as a lens that sharpens their creative vision rather than a replacement for it. The danger of data-driven design is not that it is too analytical but that it is too narrow. Optimizing for a single metric -- session length, daily logins, conversion rate -- inevitably degrades the broader experience because you start designing for the metric rather than for the player. Session length increases when you add grind. Daily logins increase when you add login streaks that punish absence. Conversion rate increases when you create artificial frustration that spending resolves. Each of these optimizations improves the target metric while making the game worse. Today we learn to build revenue projection models that account for this complexity: models where retention, engagement, and monetization interact dynamically rather than being treated as independent variables. We apply sensitivity analysis to identify which assumptions most affect the bottom line, and we discuss the judgment calls that no model can make for you.
key_concepts: Revenue modeling: LTV = sum of (ARPDAU_day_n * retention_day_n) for all days, with variations for subscription and premium models
Cohort-based revenue projection: modeling different cohort sizes, quality, and behavior over time for forecasting
Sensitivity analysis: varying key assumptions (retention, conversion rate, ARPDAU) to find which variables most impact profitability
Goodhart's Law in games: when a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a good metric because the system adapts to game it
Balancing art and science: when to follow the data, when to trust design instinct, and frameworks for resolving disagreements
Subscription and season pass models: predictable revenue stream, content obligation, player expectation management
Unit economics: fixed costs (development, infrastructure) vs variable costs (user acquisition, server scaling) and break-even analysis
Scenario modeling: best case, expected case, worst case projections with probability-weighted expected value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete revenue projection model for a F2P mobile game: varying retention, monetization rate, and ARPDAU to find break-even scenarios and optimal investment allocation
setup_requirements: Revenue modeling spreadsheet template with formulas and charts
Historical benchmark data for the target genre (provided)
Python or Excel for sensitivity analysis and scenario modeling
Case study data from a published game's financial performance

review.value: LTV = sum of (ARPDAU * retention_rate) for each day. Simplified: LTV = ARPDAU * (1 / (1 - D1_retention)) for geometric retention decay. A game with $0.05 ARPDAU and 45% D1 retention has LTV of approximately $0.09 per install.

When session length becomes the target, designers add grind. When DAU becomes the target, designers add daily login rewards that feel like obligations. Optimize for player satisfaction, measured through retention and NPS.

Testing how changes in input assumptions affect output results. If a 5% change in D1 retention causes a 40% change in LTV but a 5% change in conversion rate causes a 10% change, retention improvement should be prioritized.
The point where cumulative revenue equals cumulative costs (development + UA + operations). Games with longer break-even timelines require more capital and carry more risk.

learning_objectives: Design clear, accurate data visualizations that support decision-making
Build interactive dashboards for different stakeholder audiences
Present analytical findings in formats that drive action rather than confusion
Avoid common visualization mistakes that mislead or misrepresent data
readings: Storytelling with Data by Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic Ch. 1-5
The Visual Display of Quantitative Information by Edward Tufte, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: The most brilliant analysis is worthless if you cannot communicate it to the people who make decisions. Data visualization and stakeholder communication are the bridge between analytical insight and business action. In the game industry, your audience ranges from data-literate producers who want the statistical details to creative directors who need the bottom-line takeaway in one sentence. Each audience requires a different presentation approach but the same underlying honesty. Today we learn the principles of effective data visualization: choosing the right chart type for the data and the message, avoiding visual distortions that mislead, and building dashboards that answer stakeholders' questions before they ask them. We also practice the soft skill that analytics courses often skip: presenting findings to skeptical audiences who may not want to hear that their pet feature is hurting the game. The ability to deliver uncomfortable truths diplomatically, supported by unimpeachable data, is what separates an analytics professional who drives change from one whose reports gather dust.
key_concepts: Chart type selection: line charts for trends, bar charts for comparisons, scatter plots for correlations, heatmaps for matrices
Visual encoding: position, length, color, and area as data channels, ordered by human perceptual accuracy
Dashboard design: key metrics at top, supporting detail below, filtering and drill-down for exploration
Common visualization mistakes: truncated y-axes, misleading scales, cherry-picked time ranges, correlation-as-causation framing
Stakeholder mapping: tailoring depth and format to executive summaries, producer reports, and analyst deep-dives
Narrative structure: situation, complication, resolution format for presenting analytical findings as a story
Actionability: every chart should answer 'so what?' and every report should end with specific recommendations
Real-time dashboards: building monitoring views for live operations with alerts for anomaly detection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transforming a raw analytics dataset into three presentation formats: a one-page executive summary, a producer-level dashboard, and a detailed analyst report, comparing the information density and focus of each
setup_requirements: Data visualization tools: Python matplotlib/seaborn or Google Data Studio
Sample analytics dataset for visualization exercises
Dashboard template in Google Sheets or Looker Studio
Presentation software for stakeholder communication exercises

review.value: Line for trends over time. Bar for categorical comparison. Scatter for variable relationships. Pie charts are almost never the right choice -- human perception of angles is poor.

Starting a bar chart y-axis at a non-zero value exaggerates small differences. A change from 48% to 52% looks dramatic on a 45-55% scale but trivial on a 0-100% scale. Always include zero for bar charts.
Situation: here is the context and what we expected. Complication: here is what the data actually shows (the surprise). Resolution: here is what we recommend doing about it and expected impact.
If a stakeholder reads your report and cannot identify at least one specific action to take, the report failed. End every analysis with 'We recommend X because Y, expecting Z impact.'

class_schedule.topic: Advanced Analytics: Machine Learning for Games
learning_objectives: Apply supervised learning models to predict player behavior outcomes
Build recommendation systems for in-game content and offers
Evaluate model performance using appropriate metrics for imbalanced game datasets
Understand the limitations and risks of applying ML to player behavior
readings: Machine Learning for Game Developers (provided course reading)
Google Cloud AI for Games documentation and case studies

lecture_notes.introduction: Machine learning in game analytics moves beyond describing what happened to predicting what will happen and prescribing what to do about it. Churn prediction models flag players likely to leave before they leave, enabling proactive re-engagement. Recommendation systems suggest content and offers personalized to each player's preferences and behavior patterns. Dynamic difficulty adjustment uses real-time classification to keep players in their optimal challenge zone. However, ML is not magic. It requires clean data, careful feature engineering, appropriate algorithm selection, and honest evaluation. A churn prediction model that achieves 90% accuracy sounds impressive until you realize that predicting everyone stays also achieves 90% accuracy when only 10% of players churn. Today we build practical ML models for game analytics, learn to evaluate them honestly, and discuss the ethical guardrails that prevent predictive models from becoming manipulation engines.
key_concepts: Supervised learning for games: classification (will this player churn?) and regression (how much will this player spend?)
Feature engineering for behavior prediction: recency, frequency, monetary value (RFM), progression velocity, social activity metrics
Model selection: logistic regression (interpretable), random forests (robust), gradient boosting (accurate) for player prediction tasks
Class imbalance handling: oversampling, undersampling, SMOTE, and appropriate metrics (precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC vs accuracy)
Recommendation systems: collaborative filtering (players like you also liked...) and content-based filtering (based on your play style...)
Dynamic difficulty adjustment: real-time classification of player skill level to adjust game challenge parameters
Model deployment: serving predictions in real-time for live games vs batch processing for scheduled interventions
ML ethics in games: transparency, consent, avoiding discriminatory outcomes, and the distinction between prediction and manipulation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a gradient boosting churn prediction model, handling class imbalance with SMOTE, and evaluating with precision-recall curves to select an operating threshold that balances intervention cost against churn prevention value
setup_requirements: Python with scikit-learn, xgboost, and imbalanced-learn libraries
Player behavior dataset with 90-day history and churn labels (provided)
Jupyter notebook for model development and evaluation
Model evaluation template for documenting performance metrics

review.value: When the target variable is heavily skewed (e.g., 5% of players are spenders, 95% are not), accuracy is misleading. Use precision (of predicted spenders, how many actually spent?) and recall (of actual spenders, how many did we identify?).
ML models reveal which behavioral features are most predictive. Often the top predictors are surprising: session count in days 2-4 may predict D30 retention better than D1 session length.
Recommending items based on what similar players engaged with. 'Players who bought cosmetic set A also bought cosmetic set B.' Requires sufficient data density to find meaningful patterns.
Predicting that a player will enjoy a certain content type and recommending it serves the player. Predicting that a player is about to quit and showing them a fear-of-missing-out offer exploits them.

learning_objectives: Design monetization for premium, premium-with-DLC, and buy-to-play-with-cosmetics models
Analyze the financial dynamics of subscription-based gaming services (Game Pass, PS Plus)
Evaluate hybrid monetization approaches that combine premium purchase with live-service elements
Compare long-term player value across different monetization model families
readings: The Business of Game Design (provided industry report)
GDC Talk: 'The Economics of Game Pass' analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Free-to-play dominates mobile gaming, but the broader industry uses a diverse portfolio of monetization models. Premium games sell for an upfront price. DLC and expansion models extend the initial purchase with additional content. Subscription services like Game Pass and PS Plus fundamentally change the economics by paying developers per-download or per-play-hour rather than per-sale. Each model creates different incentives for game design, player relationship, and business sustainability. A premium game must front-load its value proposition to justify the purchase price. A DLC model must balance the base game's completeness against leaving enough desire for expansions. A subscription game must maximize engagement hours because payment correlates with play time. Today we analyze these models in depth, comparing their financial structures, player psychology implications, and design incentives. We also examine hybrid models -- premium games with cosmetic shops, F2P games with optional subscription tiers -- that attempt to capture the benefits of multiple approaches.
key_concepts: Premium model economics: development cost amortized over unit sales, pricing strategy ($40-$70 range), pre-order and launch window dynamics
DLC and season pass models: base game value proposition, DLC pricing and timing, season pass economics and player expectations
Subscription services: per-download royalties, play-hour-based compensation, discoverability benefits, and revenue predictability
Hybrid models: premium + cosmetic shop (Overwatch 2), F2P + subscription tier (Fallout 1st), base + battle pass (Destiny 2)
Player perception of value across models: perceived fairness, spending regret, content expectations by price tier
Long-tail revenue: premium games earn most revenue in the first month; live-service games earn increasing revenue over years
Platform economics: Steam revenue share (30%), console platform fees, Epic exclusivity deals, and their impact on pricing
Indie monetization: pricing for small teams, wishlists and launch discount strategy, bundling, and post-launch price management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building comparative financial models for the same game concept under three monetization strategies: premium $30, F2P with battle pass, and premium $15 with cosmetic shop, projecting 2-year revenue for each
setup_requirements: Revenue comparison spreadsheet template
Platform fee and revenue share reference data
Case study financials from published games across models (provided)
Sensitivity analysis tools for variable assumptions

review.value: Premium: 80% of lifetime revenue in first 3 months, long tail of sales during promotions. F2P: revenue builds slowly as the player base grows and matures, potentially earning more over 2-5 years.

Developers are paid based on a combination of negotiated deals and engagement metrics. Discoverability is the primary benefit -- games on subscription services are played by audiences who would never have paid full price.

Releasing DLC too early feels like content was cut from the base game. Too late and the player base has moved on. The sweet spot is 2-4 months post-launch when engaged players want more and word-of-mouth brings new buyers.
Combining premium price with aggressive microtransactions creates player resentment ('I already paid for this game'). The cosmetic-only threshold must be maintained strictly in hybrid models.

class_schedule.topic: Analytics Case Studies: Postmortems of Published Games
learning_objectives: Analyze real-world game analytics decisions through detailed case studies
Identify the specific metrics and analyses that drove pivotal business decisions
Evaluate the long-term consequences of monetization and analytics choices
Extract generalizable lessons from both successful and failed analytics implementations
readings: Deconstructor of Fun case study collection (selected articles)
GDC Postmortem talks: analytics-focused presentations (provided viewing list)

lecture_notes.introduction: Theory without practice is empty, and practice without theory is blind. Today we bridge the gap by analyzing detailed case studies of real games where analytics and monetization decisions determined success or failure. We examine games that used analytics brilliantly to identify and fix retention problems, games that made monetization mistakes that cost millions, and games that navigated the complex transition from one business model to another. Each case study follows the same structure: what was the situation, what did the data show, what decision was made, what was the outcome, and what would you have done differently? The goal is to build your judgment for the ambiguous, real-world decisions that no formula can resolve. When the data says one thing and your gut says another, when two metrics conflict, when the ethical choice reduces short-term revenue -- these are the decisions that define your career as a game analytics professional. By studying how others navigated these moments, you develop the pattern recognition that turns good analysts into great decision-makers.
key_concepts: Case study: F2P mobile game that improved D1 retention by 15% through FTUE A/B testing, cascading into 40% LTV improvement
Case study: Premium game that added loot boxes post-launch (Star Wars Battlefront II) and the regulatory and reputational fallout
Case study: Live-service game that detected economy inflation through dashboard monitoring and corrected it before player revolt
Case study: Indie game that used Steam wishlist analytics and launch timing to optimize first-week sales
Pattern recognition: common analytics red flags (declining D7 retention, falling ARPDAU, rising refund rate) and their root causes
Decision frameworks: when to trust data over intuition, when to delay decisions for more data, when to act on incomplete information
Post-mortem analysis structure: context, data, decision, outcome, lessons learned, counterfactual analysis
Building institutional knowledge: documenting analytics decisions and outcomes for organizational learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Group case study analysis: presented with a real game's analytics dashboard showing concerning trends, teams must diagnose the problem, propose interventions, and defend their recommendations
setup_requirements: Printed case study materials with analytics data
Group discussion worksheets
Access to GDC Vault for postmortem video viewing
Decision framework reference card

review.value: Improving the first-time user experience creates a cascade: higher D1 retention means more players reach D7, more reach D30, more convert to spenders. A 10% D1 improvement can translate to 30%+ LTV improvement.

Adding pay-to-win monetization to a premium game triggered regulatory investigations, mainstream media backlash, and a stock price drop. The revenue from loot boxes was a fraction of the brand damage cost.
Asking 'what would have happened if we had made a different decision?' Using historical data to estimate the outcome of the road not taken, building better decision intuition for future choices.
Level 1: basic tracking. Level 2: dashboards and reports. Level 3: A/B testing. Level 4: predictive models. Level 5: prescriptive systems that automatically optimize. Most studios are at level 2-3.

class_schedule.topic: Building an Analytics Practice: Tools, Teams, and Culture
learning_objectives: Design an analytics technology stack appropriate for different studio sizes and budgets
Structure an analytics team with clear roles, responsibilities, and workflows
Build a data-informed culture that values evidence without becoming paralyzed by analysis
Create analytics documentation that enables organizational knowledge sharing
readings: Lean Analytics by Alistair Croll and Benjamin Yoskovitz, Ch. 8-10
How to Build a Data Culture (Harvard Business Review article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Knowing analytics is necessary but not sufficient. Building an analytics practice within a game studio requires technical infrastructure, organizational design, and cultural change. Many studios collect mountains of data but lack the tools to analyze it, the people to interpret it, and the culture to act on findings that contradict senior leadership's assumptions. Today we address the organizational challenge of analytics. We examine technology stacks ranging from a solo developer using GameAnalytics free tier and Google Sheets to a large studio with a custom data warehouse, dedicated engineering team, and automated ML pipelines. We discuss team composition: when do you need a data analyst versus a data engineer versus a data scientist, and what does each role actually do day to day? Most importantly, we discuss culture. A data-informed culture is one where decisions are made by combining data with domain expertise, where it is safe to present findings that challenge assumptions, and where analytics is seen as a tool that empowers every team member rather than a surveillance system imposed from above.
key_concepts: Analytics tech stack: event SDK -> data pipeline (ETL) -> data warehouse -> query engine -> visualization layer -> alerting system
Stack by studio size: indie (GameAnalytics + Sheets), mid (Unity Analytics + BigQuery + Looker), large (custom + Snowflake + Tableau + ML platform)
Team composition: data analyst (business questions), data engineer (infrastructure), data scientist (models), analytics PM (prioritization)
Workflow design: request intake, analysis sprints, report delivery, action tracking, and closing the feedback loop
Data governance: access controls, PII handling, data retention policies, and audit trails for compliance
Building data culture: executive buy-in, self-service analytics tools, data literacy training, celebrating data-informed wins
Common failure modes: analysis paralysis, HiPPO (Highest Paid Person's Opinion) overriding data, vanity metric theater, tool obsession
Documentation: decision logs, metric definitions, analysis templates, and institutional knowledge preservation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing an analytics tech stack and team structure for three scenarios: a 5-person indie studio, a 50-person mid-size studio, and a 500-person AAA studio, with budget and timeline estimates
setup_requirements: Analytics vendor comparison matrix (provided)
Team structure templates
Budget estimation spreadsheet
Case studies of analytics team implementations

review.value: Highest Paid Person's Opinion overriding data. When the CEO says 'I think players want X' and the data shows players want Y, a healthy culture follows the data. An unhealthy culture follows the CEO.
Delaying decisions indefinitely because there is always more data to collect or another analysis to run. Set decision deadlines, accept uncertainty, and iterate based on live results.
Tools and dashboards that empower non-analysts (designers, producers, marketers) to answer routine questions without filing a request. Frees the analytics team for complex, high-value work.
A shared reference defining exactly how each metric is calculated, what data it uses, and what it means. Prevents disagreements caused by different teams calculating 'retention' differently.

class_schedule.topic: Final Project Presentations and Industry Outlook
learning_objectives: Present a comprehensive analytics and monetization project to peers and evaluators
Defend analytical conclusions and design decisions under critical questioning
Synthesize course concepts into a personal framework for ethical, data-informed game design
Identify emerging trends that will shape game analytics and monetization in the next 5 years
readings: Review all course materials for comprehensive synthesis
Industry trend reports on gaming analytics and monetization (provided reading list)

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together everything we have learned. Each student presents their capstone project -- a comprehensive analytics investigation and monetization design -- and defends their conclusions under peer and instructor questioning. Beyond the presentations, we look ahead to where game analytics and monetization are heading. Privacy regulations are tightening globally, reducing the data available for targeting and attribution. AI and machine learning are becoming more accessible, enabling smaller studios to use techniques previously reserved for companies with dedicated data science teams. Player expectations around monetization fairness are rising, driven by regulatory action and community advocacy. Blockchain and digital ownership models are being tested and debated. The subscription model continues to grow, potentially reshaping the economics of game development entirely. The analytical and ethical foundations you have built in this course will help you navigate these changes thoughtfully. The specific tools and platforms will evolve, but the ability to ask the right questions, design valid experiments, interpret data honestly, and make decisions that serve both business sustainability and player welfare -- these skills are permanent.
key_concepts: Presentation skills: structuring an analytics presentation, anticipating questions, defending methodology under scrutiny
Privacy-first analytics: designing measurement systems that work with limited tracking in a post-ATT, post-cookie world
AI-powered analytics: automated anomaly detection, natural language querying of game data, predictive model democratization
Regulatory trajectory: EU Digital Services Act, proposed US loot box legislation, potential global standards for in-game spending
Subscription economics evolution: all-you-can-play models and their impact on development incentives and game design
Web3 and digital ownership: player-owned assets, blockchain-based economies, and the unresolved questions around speculation and regulation
Emerging markets: Southeast Asia, Latin America, MENA regions with different spending behaviors and regulatory environments
Career paths in game analytics: analyst, data scientist, monetization designer, live operations manager, analytics director
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student project presentations with live Q&A, followed by group discussion of industry trends and each student's analytics philosophy statement
setup_requirements: Final project presentation materials
Peer evaluation forms
Industry trend discussion prompts
Course evaluation survey

review.value: Designing measurement that respects user privacy by default: aggregated metrics, on-device processing, differential privacy, and first-party data strategies that do not depend on cross-app tracking.

Your personal framework for how data should inform design. Should include: what you measure and why, where you draw ethical lines, how you balance data with intuition, and what you refuse to optimize.

If players pay $15/month for access to hundreds of games, developers are incentivized to maximize engagement time rather than craft focused experiences. The long-term design implications are still unfolding.

Analytics tools, platforms, and techniques evolve rapidly. The foundational skills -- statistical reasoning, ethical judgment, communication, and curiosity -- are what make you adaptable to whatever comes next.

questions.question: A mobile game has the following retention data: Day 1: 42%, Day 7: 18%, Day 30: 6%. The game acquires 10,000 new users per day at a CPI of $1.50 and has an ARPDAU of $0.08. (a) Calculate the approximate LTV per user over 30 days. (b) Is this game profitable at the current CPI? (c) What single metric would you prioritize improving and why?
questions.answer: (a) Approximate LTV: Day 0-1 avg retention ~71%, Day 1-7 avg ~30%, Day 7-30 avg ~12%. LTV = ARPDAU * (1*0.71 + 6*0.30 + 23*0.12) = $0.08 * (0.71 + 1.80 + 2.76) = $0.08 * 5.27 = $0.42. (b) No -- LTV ($0.42) < CPI ($1.50). The game loses ~$1.08 per acquired user. (c) Prioritize D1 retention. It multiplies every subsequent metric. Improving D1 from 42% to 55% would increase the retained population at every subsequent time point, and FTUE optimization is the most cost-effective intervention.

rubric.key: Reasonable LTV calculation with shown methodology
Correct profitability assessment with LTV < CPI comparison
Identifies D1 retention (or CPI) as priority with multiplicative reasoning

questions.question: You are designing an A/B test to determine whether a new tutorial improves Day 1 retention. Your baseline D1 retention is 35% and you want to detect a minimum improvement of 3 percentage points. Describe the key parameters you need to specify and explain one common mistake that could invalidate your results.
questions.answer: Key parameters: (1) Primary metric: D1 retention rate. (2) Minimum detectable effect (MDE): 3 percentage points (35% to 38%). (3) Statistical significance level: alpha = 0.05 (5% false positive rate). (4) Statistical power: 80% (20% false negative rate). (5) Required sample size: approximately 3,500-4,000 users per group. (6) Test duration: depends on daily installs to reach sample size. (7) Guardrail metrics: D7 retention, session length, crash rate. Common invalidating mistake: peeking at results before reaching the required sample size and stopping the test early when results look significant. Early results have high variance and stopping on a peak leads to overestimating the treatment effect or declaring significance that would disappear with more data.

rubric.key: Specifies at least four key parameters with correct values or ranges
Identifies guardrail metrics alongside the primary metric
Describes a genuine invalidating mistake with clear explanation

questions.question: Explain the difference between a vanity metric and an actionable metric in game analytics. Provide one example of each for a free-to-play game and explain why the vanity metric is misleading.
questions.answer: A vanity metric looks impressive but does not inform decisions or predict outcomes. An actionable metric directly connects to a business outcome and changes in response to product decisions. Vanity: total downloads. 5 million downloads sounds successful, but if D1 retention is 15%, 4.25 million people tried the game and immediately left. Downloads do not indicate game health. Actionable: D7 retention by acquisition source. This reveals which marketing channels bring players who actually enjoy the game, directly informing ad spend allocation and predicting long-term revenue potential.

rubric.key: Clear definition distinguishing vanity from actionable metrics
Valid vanity metric example with explanation of why it misleads
Valid actionable metric example with decision it informs

quizzes.topic: Monetization Design, Segmentation, and F2P Economics

questions.question: Design a battle pass system for a competitive multiplayer game. Specify: (a) tier count and completion time, (b) free vs premium track split, (c) reward types by tier range, (d) pricing strategy, and (e) one design decision ensuring fairness for non-paying players.
questions.answer: (a) 100 tiers, completable in 80-100 hours over a 10-12 week season. (b) Every tier has a free reward; premium adds a second reward per tier. Roughly 30% of total value is free, 70% premium. (c) Tiers 1-20: common cosmetics and currency. Tiers 21-60: uncommon cosmetics and emotes. Tiers 61-90: rare cosmetics and effects. Tiers 91-100: exclusive legendary set and title. No gameplay items. (d) $9.99 per season with ~1200 premium currency in rewards (next pass costs 1000), rewarding consistent players. (e) Free track includes enough premium currency over 3-4 seasons to buy one premium pass, ensuring every player has a path to premium content through play alone.

rubric.key: Realistic tier count, completion time, and season duration
Free/premium split with value distribution
Reward escalation by tier with cosmetic-only constraint
Specific fairness mechanism for non-paying players

questions.question: A mobile game studio proposes adding a gacha system to their RPG. Each pull costs $3, the 5-star rate is 0.6%, and there is a pity system at 90 pulls. With 6 equally weighted 5-star characters: (a) Calculate the worst-case cost to guarantee a specific character. (b) Name two jurisdictions where this faces scrutiny and explain why. (c) Propose one modification that improves ethics while maintaining revenue.
questions.answer: (a) Pity guarantees a 5-star at 90 pulls = $270. With 6 characters at equal rates, worst case for a specific character: 90 * 6 = 540 pulls = $1,620. With a featured banner (50/50 on featured), worst case: 180 pulls = $540. (b) Belgium: banned paid loot boxes since 2018 under gambling law. This system is illegal for real money. Netherlands: evaluates case-by-case; items with perceived real value face gambling classification. (c) Add deterministic selection: after 300 pulls on a featured banner, the player can choose any character directly. This caps maximum spend at $900, eliminates infinite worst case, gives player agency. Revenue impact is minimal because <1% of players reach that threshold.

rubric.key: Correct cost calculation accounting for character pool
Two valid jurisdictions with specific legal reasoning
Practical ethical modification with revenue impact assessment

questions.question: What is the difference between k-means clustering and manual player segmentation (e.g., Bartle types) for player analytics? Give one advantage of each approach.
questions.answer: K-means clustering discovers natural behavioral groups from actual player data without predefined categories, while manual segmentation like Bartle types uses predefined theoretical archetypes. K-means advantage: segments reflect real behavior patterns in your specific game rather than generic assumptions -- two games may have completely different natural segments. Manual advantage: categories are immediately interpretable and communicable to non-technical stakeholders ('our Achievers want...' is clearer than 'Cluster 3 users want...'), and work even before you have sufficient data for algorithmic segmentation.

rubric.key: Accurate distinction between data-driven and theory-driven segmentation
Valid advantage of k-means with reasoning
Valid advantage of manual segmentation with reasoning

quizzes.topic: Ethical Monetization, UA Analytics, and Live Operations

questions.question: A free-to-play game shows different prices for the same in-game item to different players based on their predicted spending behavior. Players with higher predicted LTV see higher prices. Is this practice ethical? Evaluate using two different ethical frameworks and state your conclusion.
questions.answer: Utilitarian analysis: personalized pricing could increase overall revenue (enabling more content development) and could subsidize free players. However, it creates unequal treatment and if discovered, destroys trust. The negative utility of player backlash likely outweighs the revenue gain. Deontological analysis: this treats players as means to extract maximum revenue rather than as autonomous agents. The practice relies on information asymmetry -- players do not know prices differ -- which violates informed consent. Conclusion: this practice is unethical. It fails the transparency test (would players consent if they knew?), creates unfair treatment, and the business risk of discovery outweighs the revenue benefit. Fixed pricing with optional premium tiers achieves revenue differentiation without deception.

rubric.key: Applies utilitarian framework with specific reasoning for and against
Applies deontological framework with autonomy/consent analysis
Clear conclusion with practical alternative

questions.question: Explain the LTV/CPI ratio and describe what happens to a game studio's finances when this ratio drops below 1.0. What are three specific actions a studio can take to improve the ratio?
questions.answer: LTV/CPI is lifetime revenue per user divided by the cost to acquire that user. When LTV/CPI < 1.0, the studio loses money on every user acquired through paid marketing -- spending $2 to acquire a user who generates $1.50 in lifetime revenue. If the studio continues paid acquisition at this ratio, it burns cash proportional to marketing spend and eventually runs out of money. Three actions: (1) Improve retention to increase LTV (FTUE optimization, content updates, re-engagement campaigns). (2) Optimize monetization to increase ARPDAU without harming retention (improve store UX, add new purchase opportunities, test pricing). (3) Reduce CPI by improving ad creative quality, targeting efficiency, or shifting budget to lower-cost channels and organic growth strategies.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of LTV/CPI ratio meaning
Clear description of financial consequences when below 1.0
Three specific, actionable strategies to improve the ratio

questions.question: What is a source-sink analysis in game economy design, and why is it critical for live-service games? Give an example of an economy imbalance and its player-facing consequence.
questions.answer: Source-sink analysis maps all ways currency enters the game economy (sources: quest rewards, daily login bonuses, event prizes) and all ways it leaves (sinks: item purchases, upgrade costs, repair fees). It is critical because live-service games add sources over time (new events, features, compensation gifts) that may not be matched by new sinks, causing gradual inflation. Example imbalance: a game adds generous event rewards (source) without new high-value items to spend them on (sink). Players accumulate millions of gold with nothing to buy. The currency becomes meaningless, new store items must be priced astronomically, and new players face an impossible gap between their wealth and veteran wealth.

rubric.key: Correct definition of source-sink analysis with examples
Explains why it is critical for live-service specifically
Concrete imbalance example with player-facing consequence

quizzes.topic: Revenue Modeling, Visualization, and Machine Learning

questions.question: Explain Goodhart's Law as applied to game analytics and give two specific examples of how optimizing a game metric can degrade the player experience. For each example, propose an alternative metric that better captures the intended outcome.
questions.answer: Goodhart's Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. Example 1: Optimizing for session length leads to adding grind (longer sessions from repetitive tasks, not enjoyment). Alternative: session satisfaction score from post-session surveys, or voluntary session extension rate (players choosing to continue past natural stopping points). Example 2: Optimizing for daily active users leads to adding punitive daily login streaks (players log in from obligation not desire, feel resentful). Alternative: weekly active users with unprompted return rate (percentage who return without being reminded by push notifications).

rubric.key: Accurate statement of Goodhart's Law in game analytics context
Two valid examples of metric optimization degrading experience
Two appropriate alternative metrics that better capture intent

questions.question: You built a churn prediction model with 92% accuracy. Your manager is impressed, but you are concerned. Why might 92% accuracy be misleading, and what alternative metrics would you use to properly evaluate the model?
questions.answer: If only 8% of players churn in the evaluation period, a model that predicts 'no one churns' achieves 92% accuracy while being completely useless for the intended purpose. This is the class imbalance problem. Better evaluation metrics: Precision: of players the model flags as churners, what percentage actually churn? (Avoids wasting re-engagement resources on non-churning players.) Recall: of players who actually churn, what percentage does the model detect? (Ensures we catch most at-risk players.) F1 Score: harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both concerns. AUC-ROC: area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, measuring the model's ability to distinguish churners from non-churners across all threshold settings.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies class imbalance as the reason accuracy is misleading
Proposes at least two appropriate alternative metrics with explanations
Demonstrates understanding of precision-recall trade-off in the game context

questions.question: What is the difference between a truncated y-axis on a bar chart and on a line chart? When is truncation acceptable and when is it misleading?
questions.answer: On a bar chart, truncating the y-axis (not starting at zero) distorts the visual comparison because bars encode data as length -- a bar that appears twice as tall should represent twice the value. A truncated bar chart makes small differences look enormous. This is almost never acceptable. On a line chart, the y-axis does not start at zero by convention because lines encode data as position, and the focus is on the trend/shape rather than absolute magnitude. Truncation on a line chart is acceptable when showing trends (a stock price chart from $95 to $105 emphasizes the meaningful variation) but misleading when the goal is to show absolute values or when the truncation hides that the variation is trivially small relative to the base value.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes bar chart (length encoding) from line chart (position encoding)
Explains when truncation is acceptable with reasoning
Explains when truncation is misleading with reasoning

quizzes.topic: Monetization Models, Case Studies, and Analytics Practice

questions.question: Compare the revenue curve of a premium $30 game with a free-to-play game with cosmetic monetization over a 2-year period. Describe the shape of each curve and explain which model is better suited for: (a) a small indie studio with limited savings, and (b) a studio that wants to build a 5-year franchise.
questions.answer: Premium $30: steep revenue spike at launch (80% of lifetime revenue in first 3 months), declining tail with occasional spikes during sales and platform promotions. Revenue is front-loaded and predictable at launch. F2P cosmetic: slow ramp-up as the player base grows, steady-state revenue proportional to DAU and conversion rate, potentially growing over years if the game retains players and adds content. Revenue is back-loaded and uncertain early. (a) Indie studio: premium is better suited because the front-loaded revenue provides immediate cash to fund the next project. F2P requires sustained investment in content and live ops before reaching profitability, which a studio with limited savings cannot sustain. (b) 5-year franchise: F2P is better suited because the ongoing revenue from a growing player base can fund continuous development, and the live-service relationship builds a community that persists across content updates rather than waiting for a sequel.

rubric.key: Accurate description of both revenue curve shapes
Correct recommendation for indie studio with financial reasoning
Correct recommendation for franchise studio with strategic reasoning

questions.question: A game's analytics dashboard shows: D1 retention dropped from 42% to 35% after the latest update, but D7 retention for the same cohort improved from 18% to 22%. How do you interpret these conflicting signals, and what analysis would you perform next?
questions.answer: These signals are not actually conflicting -- they tell a coherent story. The update likely made the early experience harder or more selective (perhaps a more honest tutorial or harder initial content), causing more players to bounce on D1. But the players who survive D1 are better matched to the game and more engaged, leading to higher D7 retention. The surviving cohort is smaller but higher quality. Next analysis: (1) Calculate absolute D7 population: old = 1000 * 0.42 * 0.18/0.42 = 180, new = 1000 * 0.35 * 0.22/0.35 = 220. Wait -- need to check if the D7 retention is of D1 survivors or of all installs. (2) Compare LTV for both cohorts to determine which scenario produces more total revenue. (3) Segment the D1 drop-off to identify which player types are leaving -- if they are all low-engagement players who would not have monetized anyway, the update is a net positive.

rubric.key: Interprets the signals as a coherent story rather than a contradiction
Identifies the quality-vs-quantity trade-off in retention
Proposes specific follow-up analyses to resolve the interpretation

questions.question: What is the 'HiPPO' problem in analytics-driven organizations, and describe one specific organizational practice that mitigates it without undermining leadership authority.
questions.answer: HiPPO stands for 'Highest Paid Person's Opinion' -- the tendency for data and analysis to be overridden by the most senior person's gut feeling or preference. This undermines the analytics function by teaching analysts that their work does not matter when it conflicts with leadership assumptions, leading to confirmation-bias analysis where analysts learn to produce data that supports predetermined conclusions. Mitigation practice: pre-registration of A/B tests. Before running any experiment, the team publicly documents the hypothesis, success criteria, and the decision that will be made based on each possible outcome. This commitment is signed off by leadership before data collection begins. When results arrive, the pre-registered decision framework removes the opportunity for post-hoc rationalization. Leadership retains authority by approving the decision framework upfront, but cannot override the data after the fact.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of HiPPO with organizational consequences
Specific practice that addresses the problem concretely
Explains how the practice preserves leadership authority

major_assignments.title: Analytics-Driven Game Optimization Report
major_assignments.description: Given a dataset of player behavior from a live game (provided), conduct a thorough analytics investigation. Identify the primary retention problem using funnel and cohort analysis, design and simulate an A/B test to address it, build a player segmentation model, and present a monetization optimization recommendation supported by data.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency in analyzing real player behavior data using statistical methods
Design a statistically valid A/B test with proper sample size calculation and guardrail metrics
Apply clustering to discover natural player segments and build targeted recommendations
Present data analysis in a clear, decision-ready format with actionable conclusions
deliverables: Retention analysis report with cohort curves, funnel visualization, and drop-off identification
A/B test design document with hypothesis, metrics, sample size calculation, expected duration, and decision criteria
Player segmentation analysis with cluster descriptions and segment-specific recommendations
Executive summary (1 page) with top 3 prioritized recommendations and expected impact
Supporting data visualizations (charts, graphs) embedded in the report with proper labeling
Python or R notebook with all analysis code (fully reproducible)

criteria: Retention curves correctly calculated and visualized by cohort with clear time labels
Primary drop-off point identified with supporting evidence and confidence in the finding
Root cause hypothesis is plausible, data-supported, and testable
Industry benchmark comparison contextualizes the findings
Hypothesis is clearly stated, falsifiable, and directly connected to the retention problem
Sample size calculation is correct with stated assumptions for significance level and power
Primary metric and at least two guardrail metrics are well-chosen and justified
Test duration, randomization strategy, and early stopping rules are specified
Clustering methodology is appropriate with clear feature selection rationale
Segments are described in actionable terms with behavioral profiles
Monetization recommendations are specific, segment-targeted, and tied to data findings
Ethical considerations for segment-targeted strategies are addressed
Visualizations are clear, properly labeled, and informative with appropriate chart types
Executive summary is concise and identifies specific actions with expected impact
Analysis is fully reproducible from the provided notebook without errors
Writing is clear, free of unsupported claims, and maintains analytical rigor
Rigorous retention and segmentation analysis of real player data
Statistically valid A/B test design with proper methodology
Actionable recommendations connected to data findings with ethical awareness
Professional presentation quality with reproducible analysis

major_assignments.title: Ethical Monetization System Design
